chunk_summary: This section discusses trace element management in pediatric cholestasis, emphasizing deficiencies, toxicity risks, and supplementation strategies during parenteral nutrition.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs and risks of trace element deficiencies in pediatric cholestasis.
Learn appropriate supplementation dosages and monitoring strategies for zinc, selenium, copper, and manganese.
Recognize the importance of balancing deficiency and toxicity risks in trace element management during parenteral nutrition.
key_facts: Zinc supplementation in pediatric cholestasis is commonly dosed at 1 mg/kg/day, with reassessment over 4–8 weeks.
Selenium deficiency can lead to cardiomyopathy, myopathy, and immune dysfunction, especially in prolonged parenteral nutrition.
Manganese accumulation during cholestasis-related parenteral nutrition can cause neurotoxicity; monitoring whole-blood manganese is recommended.
Copper deficiency can result in anemia, neutropenia, and bone abnormalities; proactive monitoring and rescue strategies are essential if copper is reduced.
Serum zinc is an inflammation-sensitive marker and may be unreliable in certain settings.
reference_quote: Deficiency can worsen appetite, immune function, wound healing, and neurodevelopment, while impaired biliary excretion can increase toxicity risk for selected elements.
document_key_topics: Supplementation strategies for zinc, selenium, copper, and manganese
Monitoring and toxicity prevention during parenteral nutrition
